topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed across my screen, bright and unwelcome. It was a post from Mr. Harrison, a photo of a sunset he’d clearly taken on a recent trip. My stomach plummeted. I stared at the image, my fingers trembling slightly as I navigated away from it. It wasn't just any teacher; he was the one who had patiently guided me through calculus, the one whose quiet encouragement had made those difficult concepts finally click. Now, he was here, sharing his life, his moments, with the world, and somehow, with me. A dull ache settled deep in my chest, a heavy, suffocating pressure that made my breath catch in my throat. I quickly minimized the app, shoving my phone into my bag as if the screen itself were radioactive. I walked to my desk, the polished wood feeling cold beneath my fingertips, and tried to focus on the textbook, but the equations swam before my eyes. Every casual smile he’d posted seemed to land like a physical blow.

[story 2]

I found myself compulsively refreshing my feed, a nervous tic I couldn't seem to break. It was Mr. Davies. His latest update was a candid shot from a weekend hike, captioned with something warm and inviting. A strange, tight knot formed just beneath my ribs, squeezing the air out of my lungs. I watched the picture, cataloging the casual ease in his posture, the way the light caught his profile. It felt like watching someone build a wall, brick by careful brick, and I was standing outside, watching the construction from a distance. When I finally closed the application, my hands were slick with sweat. I pressed my palms against my eyes, trying to scrub the image from my vision, but the warmth of the memory—the shared classroom moments, the unspoken respect—felt intensely painful now, tainted by this public display. I just wanted the quiet anonymity back, the safe distance of the lecture hall.

[story 3]

The discovery hit me during a late-night scroll. It was Ms. Evans, my English teacher. A story she’d posted detailing a book club meeting, laughing genuinely in the picture. My jaw tightened involuntarily. A sudden, sharp sting behind my eyes made me blink rapidly, trying to clear the blurry vision. I felt a profound sense of dislocation, as if the carefully constructed reality of my school life had suddenly fractured. I remember the way her words used to illuminate the dense prose, the way her presence in the room felt grounding. Now, seeing her effortlessly engaging with others online, it felt like a betrayal of that sanctuary. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my small room, my footsteps echoing loudly in the silence. My chest felt constricted, heavy, as if someone had placed a lead weight directly on my sternum. I needed air, needed distance, needed to scrub this unwelcome visibility from my perception entirely.
